                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/apiserver/api2/services:services) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!

20:31:48 06:28             [zinc]
                           ==== stderr ====

                           ==== stdout ====

WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -15 3075!
20:30:03 04:43           [compile]
                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/services/batch:batch) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!
```

it was determined that nailgun management in pants runs in disparate buildroots may be occasionally colliding due to non-strict checking of process metadata in the instance of stale pids.

we currently pass a parameter to the nailgun cmdline specifying the buildroot. this is checked during an `ng-killall` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L41) but not in the inline lifecycle management of NailgunExecutor (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L162).

the immediate remedy here appears to be to extend this checking throughout.

issue_title: Transfer Throw/Noop values across the native boundary
issue_body:
Currently, the native engine does not attempt to preserve Throw values that cross the boundary between python and rust. Part of the reason for this is that we (re)use a single buffer for the transfer of strings/utf8 data, and the completion of Runnables is batch-based.

One fix might be to make the content of the `core::Throw` on the rust side a `core::Value` representing an Exception class. The downside of this is that the rust code would need an extern to call back to python to construct exception objects in case of internal failures; but the upside is that we could preserve exceptions thrown in user code.

issue_title: New tag structure
serialize() makes a mailto href attribute unusable if it contains a query with ampersands
issue_body: `serializer.htmlserializer.serialize()` (line:248) replaces the desired `&` with `&` so that the given mailto - href is broken afterwards, see below: ``` In [1]: import bleach In [2]: bleach.clean('send') Out[2]: 'send' ``` The resulting serialized url isn't recognized by standard mail programs like _thunderbird_ or _apple mail_.

issue_title: Logging syntax does not work in Python 2
issue_body:
In commit abe42b05b4505a80fd95cbe8b284011a2aa00620 logging syntax was changed to something that does not work in Python 2:
```
self.log.debug("dropping connection: {reason}", reason=self.wasNotCleanReason)
```

You'll get see error instead of the intended error:
```
exceptions.TypeError: _log() got an unexpected keyword argument 'reason'
```

The Python2 syntax will work for both: https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging.Logger.debug

issue_title: HTTP headers causing exception in debug
issue_body:
KeyError exception experienced using asyncio, with txaio logging level at debug, when performing WAMP handshake.

A debug logging statement in [asyncio.websocket.protocol.WebSocketServerProtocol.processHandshake](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/a7c3ca3b582ea158f359e475399c06a0348b347d/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L3484) receives the HTTP header dict cast to a string. This seems to be read as part of the formatting, causing a KeyError to be raised from the logger during interpolation, as the tokens visible between the braces are not present in kwargs.

Example output:

```
2016-03-01T14:55:08 WAMP-over-WebSocket transport lost: wasClean=True, code=1011, reason="WAMP Internal Error (__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'payload_transparency')"
```

I tried to see where this had come in, but it seems to happen in both master and 0.12.1, and is not obvious following git blame back (at least to me!) - can bisect if desired. Also, not sure whether the preferred solution is to have the braces escaped or a dict passed.

issue_body:
I'm seeing two problems, one easy and minor, the other a real issue. 

1. The minor issue is that we are approximating dates to be July 2nd of the current year. Of course, that date hasn't happened yet this year, so we should make sure we don't do that. (A user reported this today).

2. The bigger problem is that the dates on the backscraped content are wrong. For example: 

https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/4344857/countiescode-home-rule-counties-power-to-regulate-the-formation-of/

That case is from 2013, but it has a date from July 2nd, 2017. I took a glance at the code for this and I didn't really follow what it was supposed to do. 

I think if we can fix the scraper code, the easiest thing will be to delete the content that we presently have and just re-scrape the website. The harder way (which would preserve the URLs we currently have) is to write a script to fix the content we have.This isn't terribly hard either, if we can create a CSV or something with the correct data. (I could probably even do this in the Python shell if I had a CSV to work with.)

issue_title: Use additional PHP repos instead of php-builds as far as possible.
issue_body:
There are both RPM and Deb repos with backported PHP binaries available.
Building PHP from source is not desired.

issue_title: Continued host clock skew issues
issue_body:
We tried to solve clock skew in #3300 by adding time synchronization to the cloud-config script, but this seems to have not done a whole lot to remedy the issue - users are still frequently reporting that their hosts are going out of sync (anecdotally, this seems to happen more often on EC2?).

We really need the expertise of our community to help us come up with a solution for continually syncing clocks via NTP.

issue_title: Azure: Switch provision scripts over to using ARM template
issue_body:
Currently, the Azure provision script uses v1 of the Azure API, which from what I understand is being deprecated in favor of [Azure Resource Manager](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/resource-group-overview/). We should pull in the ARM [template](https://github.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/pull/473) that's already been written for Deis 1.9, polish it, and use it in the project in place of the existing Python script.

I'd also like to use Terraform instead of the Azure CLI to submit the template, but it appears that Terraform doesn't yet support any ARM resources for Azure: https://terraform.io/docs/providers/azure/index.html

Using the ARM template will also address concerns such as #4332 (using an availability set for Deis) and #3323.

/cc @sgoings

issue_title: "deisctl journal" exits without warning on global units

Somehow we lost the fix from #3119, although you still see an appropriate warning for other commands:

$ deisctl status store-metadata
Unable to get status for global unit deis-store-metadata.service. Check the status on the host using systemctl.

Error: Unable to connect to global unit deis-store-metadata.service.

$ deisctl journal store-metadata
$
```

issue_title: Request: do not store domains in etcd:/deis/domains
issue_body:
Currently, when a domain is added for an app, the relationship between that app and that domain is stored in _two_ places: the database (makes sense) and etcd under the key `/deis/domains` (might not make quite as much sense).

The `deis-router`, ultimately needs to be aware of these relationships and I believe it runs confd in the background to update routes based on what's found in etcd under the `/deis/domains` key.

The problem with this is that anyone who's seeking to externalize durable platform state using the likes of RDS and S3 has no way of externalizing this little bit of configuration. It has, at least once, left me tearing down a cluster that I presumed to be stateless, only to replace it with another brand new cluster, backed by the same externalized datastores only to find that some of my routes are broken.  Deleting and re-adding the domains fixes the situation, but that's less than ideal.

Can the router source this information from the database instead?

#3486 is strikingly similar in nature, but pertains to the builder.  These two issues probably call for one common solution.

issue_title: activate no longer loads scripts in activate.d if CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV is env name, not path
issue_body:
Since 2016-06-21, as commented in the activate script, CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV is the shortest representation of how conda recognizes an environment, and CONDA_ENV_PATH is always the full path.

Thus on line 93 of the activate script, $CONDA_ENV_PATH should be used instead of $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV, otherwise the scripts are not sourced if the later is simply the env name.

issue_title: Template Creates Not Goal Seeking
issue_body:
If you run the following twice:
`az vm create -g {rg} -n {vm} --image UbuntuLTS --verbose`

You will note that in the second instance, the resulting template uses the pre-created VNET and does not include it as a resource in the generated template. Thus, if you were to use the various VM create arguments that affect the VNET or subnet, those parameters would either be ignored or the CLI would throw an error that you can't use those arguments with an existing VNET.

Should, instead, the command generate the template and make a best-faith effort to apply the arguments to the resources, even if they already exist?

issue_title: az vm extension set: autoUpgradeMinorVersion by default
issue_body:
I haven't even checked if the CLI has "az vm extension set" command (I'm mobile right now). 

But if there is any, we should make sure when we set extensions we should set autoUpgradeMinorVersion:true by default (and give users a knob to disable it). 

(This is basically the version:1.* feature in ASM)

This is good and PowerShell cmdlets do this too. 

1. It helps our users get highest minor version. 
2. It helps us reduce fragmentation on by offering users to install highest minor version available as they might be reading some old doc.

issue_title: logging.exception(HTTPException) does not print underlying chained exception
issue_body:
## Long story short

Example of badness:
```python
import logging
from aiohttp import web_exceptions   

def f():
  try:
        raise Exception('hi mom')
  except Exception as e:
        raise web_exceptions.HTTPBadRequest(reason='chaining an exception from hi mom')

try:    
    f() 
except Exception as e:   
    logging.exception(e) 
```
outputs
```
ERROR:root:chaining an exception from hi mom
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-4-6ec4e29d6e22>", line 11, in <module>
    f()
  File "<ipython-input-4-6ec4e29d6e22>", line 8, in f
    raise web_exceptions.HTTPBadRequest(reason='chaining an exception from hi mom')
aiohttp.web_exceptions.HTTPBadRequest: chaining an exception from hi mom
```
This probably has to do with `HTTPException` deriving from `Response`
## Expected behaviour
It would be nice to also see the underlying exceptions...
```python
def g():
    try:
        raise Exception('hi mom2')
    except Exception as e:
        raise ValueError('chaining a normal exception from hi mom2')
try:    
    g() 
except Exception as e:  
    logging.exception(e)
```
which outputs 
```
ERROR:root:chaining a normal exception from hi mom2
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-6-2590806aa1fb>", line 3, in g
    raise Exception('hi mom2')
Exception: hi mom2

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-6-2590806aa1fb>", line 7, in <module>
    g()
  File "<ipython-input-6-2590806aa1fb>", line 5, in g
    raise ValueError('chaining a normal exception from hi mom2')
ValueError: chaining a normal exception from hi mom2
```

aiohttp 3.3.2
python 3.7

issue_title: when session killed from other tab, notebook UI doesn't reflect change
issue_body:
Repro steps:
1. Open notebook in tab 1.
2. In another tab, visit the "Open" page, navigate to the "Running" tab.
3. Observe currently running notebook process under "Notebooks" and click "Shutdown" button.

Expected: Kernel status in tab 1 shows "Kernel is not running", "No kernel" notification appears.
Actual: Kernel status in tab 1 shows "Kernel idle", no notifications. If a cell is run, it just stays pending forever.

Under the hood, if a cell is running in tab 1 when the kernel is interrupted, there is no "shutdown_reply" message on iopub. If a cell is not running in tab 1, there is a "shutdown_reply" but it is (apparently) ignored.

issue_title: A better implementation of device placement?
issue_body:
It is known Sonnet module fails to merit the simple device placement directive, which was addressed as in issue #61 , there @kosklain offered a good solution. However there is a tiny problem in practice. Just look at the demonstrating code below:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt

class RCNNOutput(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, out_size, name = "rcnn_output"):
        super(RCNNOutput, self).__init__(name = name)
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            bf = snt.BatchFlatten(name = "bf")
            fc0 = snt.Linear(output_size = 256, name = "fc0")
            fc1 = snt.Linear(output_size = out_size, name = "fc1")
            self._seq = snt.Sequential([bf, fc0, tf.nn.relu, fc1], name = "seq")

    def _build(self, inputs):
        return self._seq(inputs)

def test():
    import numpy as np
    from tensorflow.core.framework import node_def_pb2

    num_gpus = 2

    def get_device_setter(gpu_id):
        def device_setter(op):
            _variable_ops = ["Variable", "VariableV2", "VarHandleOp"]
            node_def = op if isinstance(op, node_def_pb2.NodeDef) else op.node_def
            return  '/cpu:0' if node_def.op in _variable_ops else '/gpu:%d' % gpu_id
        return device_setter

    with tf.device(get_device_setter(0)):
        rcnn_output = RCNNOutput(4)

    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)
        ts = tf.split(t, num_gpus)

    total_outputs = []
    for i in range(num_gpus):
        with tf.device(get_device_setter(i)):
            output = rcnn_output(ts[i])
            total_outputs.append(output)

    total_outputs = tf.add_n(total_outputs)

    writer = tf.summary.FileWriter("rcnn_output_output", tf.get_default_graph())

    config = tf.ConfigProto(log_device_placement = True)

    with tf.Session(config = config) as sess:
        sess.run([tf.global_variables_initializer(), tf.local_variables_initializer()])

        v = sess.run(total_outputs)
        print(v)

    writer.close()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test()

```

The first concern is the following code snippet in the above code example:

```
    with tf.device(get_device_setter(0)):
        rcnn_output = RCNNOutput(4)
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)

```
Here 0 is just for some placeholder purpose, since the intention is to have the model parameters placed on CPU, in order to be shared across multiple GPUs. Although I could define get_device_setter in some prototype like get_device_setter(gpu_id = 0), probably someone still makes the criticism that here the sole intention is to construct everything on CPU, why a GPU ID involves? 

The criticism is tended to be a little stronger when the code snippet below is added together:

```
    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)
        ts = tf.split(t, num_gpus)

```
The intention here is we have all inputs prepared on CPU. So people will say, why you mix directives together, why you can not place them under one directive? Simply answering because Sonnet doesn't support the simple device placement directive, so it is can not be done in a harmonic way, is probably not a good answer to ease every skepticism.

So I just wonder could it be done a little nicer than the above approach, namely use a more obvious directive for a neatly placement control both for inputs preparation and model construction?

Many thanks.

issue_title: N807 false-positive for conditionally created methods
Finish EntityCreateMixin.create
issue_body:
Method [`EntityCreateMixin.create`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityCreateMixin.create) is currently a thin wrapper around [`EntityCreateMixin.create_json`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityCreateMixin.create_json). This is the case due to backwards compatibility reasons, but it needs to be changed so that NailGun can move forward. `EntitiyCreateMixin.create` should act similarly to [`EntityReadMixin.read`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityReadMixin.read). It should call `EntityCreateMixin.create_json`, create a new entity of the current type, the new entity's fields, and return the new entity.

For more thorough background info on `EntityCreateMixin`, see [this blog post](http://www.ichimonji10.name/blog/4/).

Created from SatelliteQE/robottelo#1642

issue_title: problem with version 0.78 - UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte
issue_body:
I installed from pip the version 0.78 and with some ubuntu systems (as far as I can test),  can't create the tunnel, I got the error: 
"UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte"

In the target machine the "netstat -rn" output is correct. 

If I install the package from the official repo (in my debian jessie is the 0.52 and in ubuntu 16.04 is the 0.71) the tunnel is created without issues. So I suspect that the version 0.78 maybe have some bug. 
Regards

issue_title: Stick docs link in the Github project title
Switch to pipenv
issue_body:
As we have discussed in https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-randomly/issues/135#issuecomment-398190536 there's an issue with optional dependencies.

Switching to `pipenv` will include:
1. Removing `requirements.in` and `requirements.txt`
2. Adding `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock`
3. Changing how `tox` works

That's it.
Later I will move to #135

issue_title: .net standard library with dylib - DllNotFoundException
typeof(System.Threading.Tasks.Task).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location returns Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'
issue_body:
# typeof(System.Threading.Tasks.Task).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location returns Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'

# Description
- .NET Core 2.0.0
- Windows 10 Version 1607

I am trying to use the Emit API from Roslyn from a dotnet core 2.0 console app on Windows 10. For this I have to pass Metadata References to the CSharpCompilation.Create method.

A standard way to pass these references is as follows

```C#
var metaDataReferences = new[]
            {
                MetadataReference.CreateFromFile(typeof(object).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location),
                MetadataReference.CreateFromFile(typeof(System.Linq.Enumerable).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location),
                MetadataReference.CreateFromFile(typeof(System.Threading.Tasks.Task).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location),
                MetadataReference.CreateFromFile(typeof(System.Text.StringBuilder).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location),
            };
```

The result of this statement is an array containing the following values

```
Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'
Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Linq.dll'
Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'
Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'

```

The Emit API fails with the following error
```
(5,41): error CS0246: The type or namespace name 'Task' could not be found (are you missing a using directive or an assembly reference?)
```
The reason being 
typeof(System.Threading.Tasks.Task).GetTypeInfo().Assembly.Location is returning Assembly Path='C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Private.CoreLib.dll'

Should it not return C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\2.0.0\System.Threading.Tasks.dll ?

issue_title: Marshmallow-schema'd body parameters lose 'required' and 'description' field spec
issue_body:
Consider this code:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields
from apispec import APISpec

spec = APISpec(
        title='bug',
        version="0.0.1",
        plugins=(
            'apispec.ext.marshmallow',
        )
    )

class SomeBodyParam(Schema):
    name = fields.String(required=True)
    city = fields.String()
    age = fields.Integer()

spec.add_path(
    'foo/',
    {
        'post': {
            'parameters': [
                { 'name': 'some body param',
                  'in': 'body',
                  'schema': SomeBodyParam(),
                  'description': 'Description disapears and so does required',
                  'required': True
                  }
            ]
        }
    }
)

spec.to_dict()
```

The output is:

```
{'definitions': {},
 'info': {'title': 'bug', 'version': '0.0.1'},
 'parameters': {},
 'paths': OrderedDict([('foo/',
               {'post': {'parameters': [{'in': 'body',
                   'name': 'body',
                   'required': False,
                   'schema': {'properties': {'name': {'type': 'string'}, 'city': {'type': 'string'}, 'age': {'type': 'integer', 'format': 'int32'}},
                    'required': ['name'],
                    'type': 'object'}}]}})]),
 'swagger': '2.0',
 'tags': []}
```

Bug:
- The `required` field switched from True to False
- The `description` field got lost

The `name` parameter also gets renamed, but I believe this is per-spec.

I believe the cause is here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/0.27.0/apispec/ext/marshmallow/__init__.py#L148

Adding a `, required=parameter['required'] if 'required' in parameter else False` ought to fix the required bit.  Do something similar for `description`, but need to add it to [swagger.py:fields2parameters()](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/0.27.0/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L367) signature and include it in the dict constructor in there.

That sound like a reasonable approach?

issue_title: Need a much safer default: `DjangoAuthorization` should not allow `read` access to all model object
issue_body:
Looking at `tastypie/authorization.py` (around like 133 -- master branch on 1/4/2016), the default, both `read_list` and `read_details` bypass `user.has_perm()` check, which is quite unsafe and is a bad default.

Django's Admin default is unconventional. So, I could see how it was misinterpreted.

https://docs.djangoproject.com/es/1.9/topics/auth/default/#permissions-and-authorization

    The Django admin site uses permissions as follows:
    *   ... 
    * Access to view the change list, view the “change” form and change an object is limited to users with the “change” permission for that type of object.

Essentially, "change_xyz" is the permission code for both "read" and "update". I think the better default would be following Django's Admin:


```python
diff --git a/tastypie/authorization.py b/tastypie/authorization.py
index 1d6f5aa..44b2d56 100644
--- a/tastypie/authorization.py
+++ b/tastypie/authorization.py
@@ -151,22 +151,14 @@ class DjangoAuthorization(Authorization):
         return model_klass
 
     def read_list(self, object_list, bundle):
-        klass = self.base_checks(bundle.request, object_list.model)
-
-        if klass is False:
-            return []
+        # By default, follows `ModelAdmin` "convention" to use `app.change_model`
+        # `django.contrib.auth.models.Permission` for both viewing and updating.
+        # https://docs.djangoproject.com/es/1.9/topics/auth/default/#permissions-and-authorization
 
-        # GET-style methods are always allowed.
-        return object_list
+        return self.update_list(object_list, bundle)
 
     def read_detail(self, object_list, bundle):
-        klass = self.base_checks(bundle.request, bundle.obj.__class__)
-
-        if klass is False:
-            raise Unauthorized("You are not allowed to access that resource.")
-
-        # GET-style methods are always allowed.
-        return True
+        return self.update_detail(object_list, bundle)
 
```

issue_title: In Python 3, ApiKey list in admin is not useful

issue_title: [DISCUSS] Encoder-Decoder Interface
issue_body:
I'd like to discuss about the design for encoder decoder for sequence-to-sequence models. We need such an interface so that:
1. utilities can build on these interfaces.
2. we can have a unified sequence to sequence model with pluggable encoder decoder, which speeds up development.
3. existing models can adopt the same interface for better interchangeable use.

We avoided the temptation of proposing a design first, and rather chose to wait until we have enough use cases. Now seems to be a good time to revisit this discussion.

Good starting points are the ones in sockeye, and the more simplified one in our https://github.com/dmlc/gluon-nlp/tree/master/scripts/nmt.

issue_title: Make ServerConfig inherit directly from object
issue_body:
The ServerConfig class is a kludge. It inherits directly from dict, which invites users to place anything they want into the object. But only certain pieces of data should be put in the object:

* A `base_url` attribute.
* Attributes usable by Requests, such as `verify` and `auth`.

And even with #12 addressed, the `_xdg_config_file` and `_xdg_config_dir` attributes are just still too hard to work with. It's all just a little much.

This can be addressed by making `ServerConfig` inherit directly from `object`. It could have a signature of:

```python
__init__(self, base_url=None, auth=None, verify=None, xdg_config_dir=None, xdg_config_file=None)
```

It could be used like so:

```python
cfg1 = ServerConfig(xdg_config_file='settings1.json').read()
cfg2 = ServerConfig(xdg_config_file='settings2.json').read()
```

A method called `get_request_kwargs` could be defined for use like so:

```python
cfg = get_config()
response = requests.post(
    cfg.base_url + '/some/path',
    **cfg.get_request_kwargs()
)
```

This may not work in all situations, but it should be a reasonable basis to build upon. We'll have a well-defined set of valid instance attributes and a good way of working with multiple configuration files.

issue_title: Capture and process salt state return info to output failed states in traceback
issue_body: When a state fails, watchmaker throws a traceback. That traceback is often viewed as and reported as the error message, but it is just indicative of a failed state. The actual failure is in the debug log output, earlier in the log stream. The change proposed here would be to capture the return info of the salt state output and process it, searching for failed states and reporting them in the traceback, to improve the message in the log.

issue_title: Interest in pre-initializing dictionary?
issue_body:
Hi all -- I implemented functionality to pre-initialize the dictionary, so that it can be reused in subsequent runs over the same training dataset (useful for eg parameter tuning).  It's called by

```
fasttext init-dictionary -input myinput -output myoutput
```
and saves the dictionary to `myoutput.dict`.  

It's subsequently loaded by
```
fasttext supervised -input myinput -output myoutput -dictionary myoutput.dict
```

At the moment, it's tangled up with a bunch of other edits I've made, but if there's interest I could pull it out and open a PR.  Any thoughts?  I didn't want to spend the ~hour refactoring and testing if people don't think this is useful.

~ Ben

issue_title: SSL certificate validation on Python 2.7.3 is broken by 0.10.2
issue_body:
https://github.com/httplib2/httplib2/commit/40cbdcc8586f2292fa0e76a3e8c012f0cc9ed919 introduced a dependency on `ssl.CertificateError` which isn't available in Python 2.7.3. This means that calls to _ssl_wrap_socket will always raise `httplib2.CertificateValidationUnsupported`:

```
$ python2
iPython 2.7.3 (default, Oct 26 2016, 21:01:49) 
[GCC 4.6.3] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import ssl
>>> ssl.CertificateError
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'CertificateError'
>>> import httplib2
>>> httplib2._ssl_wrap_socket(None, None, None, None, None, None, None)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/srv/jenkins/jobs/GCE-Publish-Daily/workspace/wagon_venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/httplib2/__init__.py", line 101, in _ssl_wrap_socket
    "SSL certificate validation is not supported without "
httplib2.CertificateValidationUnsupported: SSL certificate validation is not supported without the ssl module installed. To avoid this error, install the ssl module, or explicity disable validation.
```

issue_title: FindNewPython's checks breaks FindPythonLibs
issue_body:
A recent change to pybind11 does [checks against a python interpreter](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/commit/928fff649fa52ebc7b43860a4914f68e320d2bb2 ). The verification done for FindPythonLibs doesn’t apply in the case of [superbuilt python](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKExamples/blob/master/Superbuild/External-Python.cmake) (ExternalProject_Add) since the PYTHON_EXECUTABLE isn't ready until compile time.

In my circumstances, I already have ``${PYTHON_LIBRARY}`` and ``${PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIR}`` set.  CMake's [FindPythonLibs](https://github.com/Kitware/CMake/blob/master/Modules/FindPythonLibs.cmake) allows you to specify your versions:

> If you'd like to specify the installation of Python to use, you should
modify the following cache variables:

> ```
>      PYTHON_LIBRARY             - path to the python library
>      PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIR         - path to where Python.h is found
> ```

Expected behavior: ``FindPythonLibsNew.cmake`` should skip ``execute_process(COMMAND "${PYTHON_EXECUTABLE}" "-c"`` and/or fallback to ``find_package(PythonLibs)`` behavior if ``PYTHON_LIBRARY`` and ``PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIR`` is already set.

issue_title: Integrate the token network contract
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Part 2/2 of #1025.

> At the moment whenever we open a channel we create a smart contract per channel. We went with this implementation for simplicity reasons in the beginning but it is not optimal as it increases gas costs for channel opening due to the CREATE operation.

Should be done after https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1171

## Tasklist

- [ ] Integrate the [token network](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/blob/7d183081bcd6df06728bdb6b8da581e68c39dbc1/raiden_contracts/contracts/TokenNetwork.sol) into the raiden codebase
- [ ] Remove the old netting channel code

issue_title: Switch channel implementation to one global contract per token
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment whenever we open a channel we create a smart contract per channel. We went with this implementation for simplicity reasons in the beginning but it is not optimal as it increases gas costs for channel opening due to the `CREATE` operation.

**EDIT**: This has been split into 2 issues:

#1171 and #1172

## Solution

Have a global contract managing all channels per token.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Replace the `NettingChannelLibrary` and `NettingChannelContract` with an equivalent contract managing all channels for a token.
- [ ] Make adjustments to the code so that it works with the new contracts.
- [ ] Change the [relevant spec entry](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b3fe38fd6f5939aed8e6d0b8142144657d98e4f8/docs/spec.rst#one-contract-per-channel) to reflect the new implementation and the reasoning behind it.
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: Should be possible to persist intermediate states in complex `update`
issue_body:
In providers like Kubernetes, `update`'ing a single resource may actually involve several discrete steps, each of which may fail. In situations where these steps are well-known (_e.g._, deploying a Kubernetes `Service` object), it is possible to greatly simplify the debugging process simply by explaining in which intermediate step the provisioning process failed.

For Pulumi, there are several possibilities for where this information might be exposed:

* In the CLI, it should be possible to display progress on intermediate updates (_e.g._, "x of y containers running in pod", "container failed with status x", "Pod replicated x out of y times", and so on). In the case of Kubernetes, this information is mostly exposed in the API, and it is therefore a matter of plumbing that information in, so that it's rendered correctly in the CLI.
* The engine could stream these intermediate updates to the Pulumi service, thereby allowing it to be rendered in the Pulumi console. Hence, if `pulumi` is run as part of a CI system, it would be possible to recover this information for diagnostic purposes simply by visiting the console.

issue_title: Possible performance improvements
issue_body:
I looked at `psparser.py` file and sow `bytesindex` function which purporse is to replace `byteobj[from:to]` and more precisely `byteobj[idx]` to return the same on Python 2 and 3.

From my understanding and experiments the only difference between Python 2 and 3 in regards to getting element or slice from byteobject is that in Python 3 when you get single element you receive integer instead of bytestrig. When you get slice is the same in both Python 2 and 3.

Now the implementation of `bytesindex` differ from how slices works if `to` is a negative value. In current implementation if `-1` (or any other negative value) is passed as `to` then it will be to the end of the bytestring instead to the end minus one byte (or the exact number of bytes). Because of that implementation detail all usages of `bytesindex` where all bytes to the end of the bytestring need to be get are misleading because it uses `-1` as argument.

The possible performance improvements can be because of reduced function calls if instead of the function proper slice is used. Also will be more obvious the the reader what exactly data are get from the bytestring.

平常调度爬虫的时候会 curl  ..... -a arg1=value1  -a arg2=value2  -a arg3=value3 传参数，在使用 Gerapy的时候，调度爬虫，点run后就直接提交运行了。

这个虽然可以直接修改文件里面的参数来做这样的操作，但是还是要修改文件代码，总觉得有点麻烦。

要是能直接在调度的时候添加的话就挺好的。

issue_title: Allow option for napoleon docs rather than sphinx
issue_body:
The plain-text docstrings (i.e. accessed from ipython) are sometimes hard to read due to sphinx formatting. A small aspect of this was addressed in response to this issue here: https://github.com/seequent/properties/issues/8 but overall, docs are still sphinx through-and-through.

It would be great to enable easier to read docs with Napolen - https://sphinxcontrib-napoleon.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ - this could mean a couple of things. Either (1) have a check for `__IPTYTHON__` (or maybe just a flag on the HasProperties class) that causes napolean docs to be built instead of sphinx, or (2) only build napoleon docs and use the sphinx extension to build docs from there.
